metadata.summary: Identify emerging market trends using STEEP framework and weighted Trend Strength Score. Filter signal from noise to position entry at optimal adoption-curve stage with counter-signal critic.

content.title: Trend Analysis
content.summary: A systematic pipeline for identifying and evaluating emerging market trends before acting on them. Uses the STEEP framework (Social/Technology/Economic/Environmental/Political) and a weighted 5-criterion Trend Strength Score to filter signal from noise and position entry at the optimal adoption-curve stage.

why.p: Entrepreneurs either chase every trend (shiny-object syndrome) or ignore trends completely. Without a structured scoring method — with a counter-signal critic and a mandatory two-week sanity gap — agents default to "pursue" on every trend and invent supporting numbers. The fan-out/fan-in pipeline grounds every evidence row in a cited source URL.

list.item: Pre-niche selection: deciding if a vertical has tailwind before committing 6+ months.
Quarterly portfolio review: re-scoring active bets against fresh signals.
New-product wedge hunting: pairing STEEP output with personal-fit filter.
Competitor threat scan: detecting stage shifts (early → mainstream → decline).
Content/SEO planning: catching search-term inflection before incumbents arrive.
Sub-week tactical decisions (today's content topic, a single ad creative).
Validating an already-paying problem — customer interviews beat trend extraction here.
Deeply local B2B niches where no public signal source exists (Trends/HN/PH all blank).
When you already have product-market fit — trend-hopping is the failure mode this warns against.

rule.statement: Classify every trend as Fad (weeks-months), Trend (2-5 years), Megatrend (decades), or Shift (permanent) before scoring it — solopreneur sweet spot is the Trend category.
rule.rationale: Fads do not sustain a business. Megatrends and Shifts are too broad to carve a defensible niche quickly. 2-5 year trends offer the largest opportunity window relative to required investment.

list.item: Fad: weeks to months (fidget spinners, NFT art)
Trend: 2-5 years (remote work tooling, no-code platforms)
Megatrend: decades (aging population, climate adaptation)
Shift: permanent (internet adoption, mobile-first)

rule.statement: Enter just before mainstream adoption — after the Early Adopter stage confirms demand but before incumbents have locked distribution.
rule.rationale: Innovator stage: too early, no market. Late mainstream: commoditization and shrinking margins. The Early-to-Mainstream inflection is where CAC is still low and pricing power exists.

list.item: Innovator: define category — risk: too early, no market
Early adopter: high margins — risk: must educate the market
Early mainstream: growth — risk: competition increasing (optimal entry point)
Late mainstream: volume — risk: commoditization
Decline: niche/legacy — risk: shrinking market

rule.statement: Apply the STEEP framework (Social, Technology, Economic, Environmental, Political) to every trend before scoring; accept "n/a" for irrelevant dimensions rather than padding.
rule.rationale: Agents fill all five STEEP cells even when Environmental is irrelevant — forcing irrelevant cells produces noise that inflates the score. Explicit "n/a" is honest and faster to audit.

list.item: Social: How are behaviors and values changing? Which demographics are shifting?
Technology: What new tech enables this trend? What capabilities became cheap enough?
Economic: What financial forces drive it? What cost arbitrages exist?
Environmental: How does sustainability affect adoption? Are there regulatory tailwinds?
Political: What regulations impact it? Which governments are accelerating or blocking?

rule.statement: Compute the weighted Trend Strength Score (growth rate 25%, adoption stage 20%, incumbent count 15%, investment activity 20%, personal fit 20%) before the counter-signal critic runs; treat any score above 4.0 as requiring an adversarial review pass.
rule.rationale: Bullish-only pipelines recommend "pursue" on every trend. A high score without a counter-signal critic is a confirmation bias loop.

example.note: AI Agents trend scored in 2024: Google Trends +500% in 6 months (growth 5/5), Early mainstream stage (stage 4/5), 50+ incumbents (incumbents 2/5), $2B+ funding (investment 5/5), personal fit varies by team (fit 3/5). Weighted total: 4.1/5 — triggers adversarial review.
Accepting a 5/5 score on all criteria without a single cited source URL — the rubric becomes a self-reinforcing fantasy, not a decision tool.

list.item: Hacker News (Algolia API): developer sentiment and new-tool discussion — free, no key, 30-day window
Product Hunt (GraphQL API): new products and category emergence — free tier available
GitHub (REST API): star velocity as adoption proxy — filter by created date, sort by stars
arXiv (pip install arxiv): paper counts as research-momentum signal — category + date filter
VC blogs (RSS): investment thesis patterns — lagging but high-signal (a16z Big Ideas, etc.)

rule.statement: Use GitHub star velocity (new repos in last 180 days + top star count) as an early technology-trend signal, not absolute star count.
rule.rationale: Absolute star count rewards incumbents. Star velocity captures acceleration — the signal that matters for timing entry.

list.item: Google Trends (pytrends): search interest over time — smoothed on low-volume terms, splits on term renames
Exploding Topics: curated emerging topics with growth rate — good seed source, Pro plan has API
Crunchbase / PitchBook: funding velocity — paid API, hard gate if key missing (fail loudly)
LinkedIn Jobs / Indeed: skill keyword volume — hiring volume is strongest lagging-validation signal
Gartner Hype Cycle / Forrester: authoritative for B2B but lag inflection by 12-18 months

rule.statement: Score deltas, not absolutes: last-month vs this-month change in HN hits / star velocity beats raw counts.
rule.rationale: Absolute counts reward historical incumbents. Month-over-month deltas surface accelerating momentum regardless of baseline.

list.item: Reddit (PRAW): subreddit subscriber growth and post velocity — free API, strong for validating intensity
TikTok Creative Center: emerging consumer behaviors — limited API, scrape with care
Amazon bestsellers: product category shifts — no API, manual or Playwright scrape
App Store rankings: category leader emergence — Apptopia/Sensor Tower API for trend data

antipattern.description: Letting the agent pick its own sources for numeric evidence.
antipattern.reason: Unconstrained source selection collapses to "search Google, cite first SEO-spam page". Pin the allow-list of authoritative sources in the system prompt.

antipattern.description: Using Google Trends on a low-volume term without checking if the term was recently renamed.
antipattern.reason: "AI agent" and "agentic AI" split the trend curve. Synonym-merge before scoring to avoid treating the same trend as two different signals.

antipattern.description: Treating a single viral HN or Reddit thread as a trend confirmation.
antipattern.reason: Require >= 3 independent sources before stage-promoting a trend. One viral post is a data point, not a trend.

rule.statement: Run trend analysis as a fan-out/fan-in pipeline: one orchestrator dispatches per-source collectors in parallel; a synthesizer normalizes them into STEEP and Trend-Strength tables; a counter-signal critic runs before the recommendation is written.
rule.rationale: Serial collection is slow. The counter-signal critic is non-negotiable — a bullish-only pipeline will recommend "pursue" on every trend regardless of underlying strength.

list.item: faion-market-researcher-agent: orchestrator, owns STEEP + scoring rubric and final report
web-search-collector (Haiku): runs WebSearch/WebFetch against signal sources, returns JSON rows only — no reasoning
data-quantifier (Sonnet): converts raw source dumps into Google Trends deltas, GitHub star-velocity, funding totals
counter-signal-critic (Sonnet): adversarial agent — searches for cooling indicators, failed precedents, regulatory blockers
personal-fit-scorer (Haiku): applies the user's skill/audience/interest filter from a memory file

example.note: Orchestrator prompt — strict JSON schema with source_url required field:
example.code: You are the trend-analysis orchestrator. Analyze trend "{TREND}". Output ONLY this JSON schema: { "trend": "...", "category": "fad|trend|megatrend|shift", "stage": "innovator|early|mainstream|decline", "evidence": [{"metric":"","value":"","yoy":"","source_url":""}], "steep": {"social":"","tech":"","econ":"","env":"","political":""}, "score": {"growth":1-5,"stage":1-5,"incumbents":1-5,"investment":1-5,"fit":1-5,"weighted_total":0-5}, "counter_signals": ["..."], "recommendation": "pursue|monitor|skip", "next_review": "YYYY-MM-DD" } Rules: every numeric field MUST cite source_url. If unknown, write "unknown" — never estimate.

example.note: Counter-signal critic prompt — adversarial, returns 3-7 bullets with source URLs:
example.code: You are the counter-signal critic. Given trend "{TREND}" and the prior bullish report, search for: (a) declining adjacent metrics, (b) regulatory threats, (c) prior cycle failures with same shape, (d) incumbent moats that prevent entry. Return 3-7 bullets, each with a source URL.

rule.statement: Run trend analysis monthly on a recurring cadence — first run alone is near-useless. Trend strength is read from the slope across runs.
rule.rationale: A single snapshot cannot distinguish a spike from a trend. Monthly deltas reveal acceleration or cooling. First-run data is baseline only.

rule.statement: Apply the personal-fit filter before scoring, not after. Filter 100 trends to 10 by fit, then score the 10.
rule.rationale: Scoring 100 trends and rejecting on fit at the end wastes 90% of scoring compute and tempts the agent to recommend fit-mismatched trends anyway.

rule.statement: Enforce a two-week sanity gap — never act on a trend the same session it was discovered. Log it, re-evaluate on the next dashboard run.
rule.rationale: Recency bias causes immediate action on trends that cool within 2 weeks. The gap filters ephemeral spikes from sustained signals.

rule.statement: Anchor every number to a URL. Reject unsourced rows from agent output — this kills the #1 hallucination class.
rule.rationale: LLMs invent CAGRs and funding totals with full confidence. Force JSON schema with source_url required; reject rows without it.

rule.statement: Keep a kill-list memory. Trends marked "skip" with reason persist across sessions, so the orchestrator does not re-recommend them.
rule.rationale: Without memory, the pipeline surfaces the same dismissed trends monthly, wasting compute and user attention.

rule.statement: Collapse near-duplicate terms before scoring. "AI agent", "agentic AI", "AI agents" point at the same trend — synonym-merge.
rule.rationale: Without merging, the same trend fragments across multiple signal sources, inflating the apparent breadth of supporting evidence.

list.item: Numeric hallucination: LLMs invent CAGRs and funding totals. Force JSON schema with source_url required; reject rows without it.
Stale training data: model reports 2023 funding rounds as current. Always wire WebSearch/WebFetch and check current_date against returned URLs.
STEEP padding: agents fill all five cells even when Environmental is irrelevant. Accept "n/a" as valid.
Recommendation drift toward "pursue": default LLM tone is optimistic. Counter-agent + forced skip budget (reject >= 40% of trends per run) counteract this.
Date-window mismatch: one source returns 30-day, another YoY. Normalize windows in the collector layer.
Token blowup: never paste full HTML/JSON into the synthesizer — collectors must return <= 20-row distilled records.

section.name: failures-and-limitations
section.p: Survivorship bias in sources: HN/PH/Crunchbase over-index on dev-tooling and SF-flavored startups; consumer-physical and emerging-market trends barely register.
Lagging signal mistaken for leading: Gartner Hype Cycle and McKinsey reports trail the actual inflection by 12-18 months.
Google Trends noise: Tiny-volume terms get smoothed to zero; new terms have no historical baseline; a term rename (e.g., "AI agent" vs "agentic AI") splits the curve.
STEEP scoring is subjective: Two analysts score the same trend 3.0 and 4.5 — without rubric anchors, the framework becomes self-reinforcing.
Counter-trend blindness: The framework rewards "X is rising" but rarely surfaces "Y collapsed adjacent to X" (e.g., crypto winter killing Web3 dev tools).
LLM hallucinated stats: When agents answer "what is the CAGR of X?" without grounding, they confidently invent numbers — every figure must be source-anchored.

template.purpose: Trend Analysis Report: summary, evidence table, STEEP, competitive landscape, recommendation
Multi-source collector: Google Trends (pytrends), HN Algolia, GitHub repo search
